codeSnapshot.label: random_search.py
codeSnapshot.code:
from sklearn.model_selection import RandomizedSearchCV
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.datasets import make_classification
from scipy.stats import uniform, loguniform, randint
import numpy as np

X, y = make_classification(n_samples=1000, n_features=20, random_state=42)

# Random search: sample hyperparameter configurations from distributions
# Key insight (Bergstra & Bengio, 2012):
# If only 2 of d hyperparameters truly matter, random search covers
# each effective dimension independently — grid search wastes coverage
# on the d-2 unimportant dimensions

# Continuous distributions for continuous hyperparameters
param_distributions = {
    'learning_rate':    loguniform(1e-4, 1e-1),  # log-uniform in [1e-4, 1e-1]
    'n_estimators':     randint(50, 500),          # uniform integer in [50, 500)
    'max_depth':        randint(2, 10),             # integer [2, 10)
    'subsample':        uniform(0.5, 0.5),          # uniform in [0.5, 1.0]
    'min_samples_leaf': randint(1, 20),
    'max_features':     uniform(0.3, 0.7),          # fraction of features
}

# RandomizedSearchCV: samples n_iter configurations at random from distributions
random_search = RandomizedSearchCV(
    estimator=GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=42),
    param_distributions=param_distributions,
    n_iter=50,              # 50 random configs × 5 folds = 250 fits
    cv=5,
    scoring='accuracy',
    n_jobs=-1,
    random_state=42,        # reproducible sampling
    verbose=1,
    refit=True,
)
random_search.fit(X, y)

print(f'Best params: {random_search.best_params_}')
print(f'Best CV score: {random_search.best_score_:.4f}')

# Hyperband (Li et al., 2018) — resource-adaptive random search
# Uses successive halving: evaluate many configs cheaply, promote best half,
# repeat with more resources for survivors
# Implemented in: Ray Tune, Optuna (HyperbandPruner), HuggingFace Trainer
from optuna import create_study
from optuna.pruners import HyperbandPruner
from optuna.samplers import TPESampler  # Bayesian-informed random sampling

# Optuna trial: define the search space with suggest_* methods
def objective(trial):
    lr = trial.suggest_float('lr', 1e-4, 1e-1, log=True)    # log-uniform
    n_est = trial.suggest_int('n_estimators', 50, 500)
    max_d = trial.suggest_int('max_depth', 2, 10)
    model = GradientBoostingClassifier(learning_rate=lr, n_estimators=n_est,
                                       max_depth=max_d, random_state=42)
    return model.fit(X, y).score(X, y)  # simplified; use CV in practice

study = create_study(direction='maximize', sampler=TPESampler())
study.optimize(objective, n_trials=20)
print(f'Best trial value: {study.best_value:.4f}')

eli3: Random search randomly samples hyperparameter configurations from a range you specify (e.g., learning rate between 0.0001 and 0.1, chosen randomly from a log-uniform distribution), instead of trying every value on a fixed grid.

The key insight from Bergstra & Bengio (2012): most hyperparameters don't actually matter much — only 1-3 of them have large effects. If you have 9 hyperparameters but only 2 matter, random search efficiently explores different values for those 2, while grid search wastes 7 dimensions trying every combination of unimportant ones.

Random search with 50 tries is usually better than grid search with 50 tries — and often comparable to grid search with 500 tries. The reason: random search samples continuously, so it can find values between fixed grid points.

whenWhy.title: High-dimensional hyperparameter spaces
whenWhy.body: Bergstra & Bengio (2012) proved that random search dominates grid search when the effective dimensionality (number of hyperparameters that truly matter) is much less than the total number of hyperparameters d. For d=9 dimensions where only 2 matter: a 9D grid with 3^9=19,683 points sees only 3 distinct values per important dimension; the same budget of 19,683 random samples sees ~19,683 distinct values per dimension. Random search is the default starting point for HPO before trying Bayesian methods.

whenWhy.title: Continuous hyperparameter distributions
whenWhy.body: Grid search requires discretizing continuous hyperparameters. Random search samples from continuous distributions (loguniform for learning rate, uniform for dropout rate). This means random search can find values between grid points — critical for sensitive hyperparameters where the optimum is at 0.0037 but the grid only checks 0.001 and 0.01. scipy.stats.loguniform(a, b) samples from a log-uniform distribution — the standard prior for scale-invariant hyperparameters like learning rate.

whenWhy.title: Hyperband: resource-adaptive random search
whenWhy.body: Hyperband (Li et al., 2018) extends random search with early stopping: start many configurations with a small resource budget (few training epochs), keep only the top fraction, double the budget, repeat. This successive halving eliminates poor configurations early. Hyperband is asymptotically optimal for any fixed budget — it automatically trades off exploring many configurations cheaply vs exploiting promising ones with more compute. Implemented in Ray Tune, Optuna, HuggingFace Trainer.

deepDiveIntro: Bergstra and Bengio (2012) showed that when only a small number of hyperparameters truly influence the objective, random search is much more efficient than grid search. Grid search wastes most of its evaluations on unimportant dimensions, exploring only a few distinct values of the important ones. Random search, by sampling independently in each dimension, sees many more distinct values for the important hyperparameters within the same total budget. [1]

Successive Halving extends random search with early stopping: start many configurations cheaply, keep the top fraction, double the budget, and repeat. Hyperband runs several brackets of Successive Halving simultaneously to avoid the need to choose the initial number of configurations upfront. The total computational cost is logarithmic in the number of starting configurations rather than linear, making it much more efficient than full training on all candidates. [2]

deepDive.heading: Sampling Distributions for Hyperparameters
deepDive.body: Choice of prior distribution is critical for random search quality. Learning rate: loguniform — the effect of multiplying by 10 is the same regardless of scale (0.0001→0.001 same effect as 0.001→0.01). Regularization (weight decay, C in SVM): loguniform. Dropout rate, subsample, momentum: uniform in [a,b] — linear sensitivity. Integer hyperparameters (n_estimators, layers): randint. Categorical (optimizer, activation): random.choice. scipy.stats distributions are directly compatible with sklearn RandomizedSearchCV. Optuna's suggest_float(log=True) implements loguniform; suggest_int implements randint with uniform or log-uniform spacing. [1]

deepDive.heading: Successive Halving and Hyperband
deepDive.body: Successive Halving starts with many configurations given a small resource budget, evaluates all of them, keeps only the top fraction, multiplies the budget by a fixed factor, and repeats. As a concrete example with 81 starting configurations, a halving factor of 3, and a maximum of 100 epochs: round 1 trains all 81 for roughly 1 epoch, round 2 trains the surviving 27 for 3 epochs, and so on until a single winner is trained for 81 epochs. The total compute is several times less than training all 81 configurations to completion. Hyperband runs multiple such brackets simultaneously with different starting counts. [2]

deepDive.heading: When Random Search Underperforms
deepDive.body: Random search assumes hyperparameter importance is known (good prior distributions). If the prior is wrong (e.g., uniform over [0.1, 100] for learning rate when the optimum is 1e-4), random search wastes evaluations. Bayesian optimization builds a surrogate model to learn where to sample next — more efficient than random for low-budget (<50 evaluation) settings. For deep learning: learning rate + batch size are nearly always the two most important hyperparameters — a focused random search over just these two often outperforms random search over all hyperparameters. [1]

youtubeLabel: Random Search vs Grid Search — Which Is Better?
takeaways: Random search dominates grid search when only a few hyperparameters truly matter (Bergstra & Bengio). [1]
Use loguniform for learning rate, regularization; uniform for rates/fractions; randint for integers. [1]
50 random configurations typically suffice for 5-10 hyperparameter problems (budget: 50×k_folds fits). [1]
Hyperband (successive halving): train many configs cheaply, promote best → O(log(n)) speedup. [2]
Advantage over grid: samples continuously — finds values between fixed grid points. [1]

edgeCases.q: How many random search iterations are needed to find the optimum with high probability?
edgeCases.a: If the top p fraction of configurations are 'good' (i.e., within ε of optimal performance), then N = log(1-δ)/log(1-p) iterations are needed to find at least one good configuration with probability ≥1-δ. Example: top 5% are good (p=0.05), want 95% probability (δ=0.05): N = log(0.05)/log(0.95) ≈ 59 iterations. Top 10% good, 95% probability: N = log(0.05)/log(0.90) ≈ 29 iterations. This simple probabilistic argument justifies the empirical finding that 50-100 random configurations usually suffices for most practical problems. Bayesian optimization reduces this number by intelligently targeting good regions of the space.

edgeCases.q: How should you handle correlated hyperparameters in random search?
edgeCases.a: Standard random search samples each hyperparameter independently from its marginal distribution, ignoring correlations. If hyperparameters are strongly correlated (e.g., in transformers: model_dim must equal num_heads × head_dim), independent sampling produces many invalid configurations (model_dim=256, num_heads=12, head_dim=20 — doesn't divide evenly). Solutions: (1) Conditional search spaces — only sample head_dim given model_dim: head_dim = model_dim/num_heads. Optuna's conditional hyperparameters support this via trial.suggest_* based on other params. (2) Template-based search: define valid architecture templates and search over template indices rather than raw parameters. Optuna, Ray Tune, and NNI all support conditional parameter spaces.

sources.label: Bergstra & Bengio (2012) — Random Search for Hyper-Parameter Optimization, JMLR
Li et al. (2018) — Hyperband: A Novel Bandit-Based Approach to Hyperparameter Optimization, JMLR
